reviews.review_title: Not sure it's for me but...
reviews.review_description:
I know this is still in early release and some of the secondary features such as meditation and other activities are not available yet. I plan to update my review once they are because it may have a pretty big impact on my personal opinion of this.

The main feature, relying on cognitive behavioral therapy pushes you to identify negative thoughts and explore connections between those thoughts and your behaviors in an effort to shift those thoughts and feelings into something more productive.

I find myself in a position right now where there isn't a lot of thought or behavior that can really help with the situation causing my stress. While I think this approach could be helpful for a lot of people, especially when someone's issues fall into the stereotypical "first world problems" category, 

While I do have existential feelings, the problems I'm currently encountering are with things outside of my control that I don't believe the main feature in this app are necessarily suited to deal with. 

Having said that, the experience of using it was interesting and I think there are probably a lot of people that could find a sense of comfort and emotional relief from using it under the right circumstances and I look forward to testing some of the other features once they come online.

developer_response.body:
Hey ThePromee, your balanced take means a lot—it's exactly the kind of insight that helps us iterate as VR devs building for real-life complexities!  

We get that early releases like ours focus on core CBT to tackle thought-behavior loops, and external stressors (those uncontrollable curveballs) can feel out of reach. But the immersive VR setup is meant to create that pocket of comfort, even amid chaos, and we're excited you're finding it intriguing.

The good news? Our newest update rolls out those secondary features you mentioned—meditation modules still in progress, but guided activities, and adaptive scenarios tailored for broader stress relief, such as Emotional Diary, are now live and ready to explore. For situations beyond the basics, premium unlocks deeper tools like relapse prevention boosters and personalized coping strategies. Give the update a whirl in VR; it might just shift your perspective under the right circumstances.

If you need someone to vent to or something similar to that, this is a great tool for that. While it has its problems, (primarily issues with AI’s limitations) it does a pretty decent job at making the player (you) comfortable. As it stands right now, it’s a great supplementary tool for those in therapy who need extra help outside of sessions, or for those who need some help, but can’t find a good therapist to boot. In its current early access state (as of me reviewing) it is ripe with potential, it is not perfect yet, but that’s okay. The primary principle it uses is only Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) which as a concept thankfully does tend to help with a lot of everyday problems like anxiety and dealing with stress. It does have some knowledge gaps in knowledge, but in future updates that will likely be resolved. (I will come back to update this if/when it does to accommodate.)

reviews.review_title: This App Violates Federal and State Law
reviews.review_description:
This application violates U.S. state and federal law by engaging in the unlicensed practice of mental health counseling and psychotherapy.

The app “EvaAI,” developed by Impaq LLC FZ and available on the Meta Quest platform, explicitly markets itself as providing therapeutic support, including cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) techniques, mental health screenings for depression and anxiety, and behavioral guidance. These are not general wellness features. They are legally defined clinical services, and delivering them without licensure constitutes a violation of multiple state practice acts across the United States.

Marketing language such as “Eva is here for you… to help you deeply explore the patterns of your thinking and guide you toward changing behaviors” and “discover fascinating insights about psychotherapy and VR therapy” unequivocally positions EvaAI as a provider of psychological services. This language, along with user testimonials stating that Eva offers “therapy,” “CBT tips,” and “mental health tests,” confirms that the app is operating well within the legal scope of psychotherapy.

Under U.S. law, any person or entity offering psychotherapy, psychological diagnosis, counseling, or behavioral intervention must be licensed by the appropriate regulatory board in the state where services are offered (see e.g., Texas Occupations Code § 503.001, California Business and Professions Code § 2903, Florida Statutes § 491.003). The absence of licensed clinical oversight, supervision, or disclaimers about the limits of the AI's functionality makes this not only deceptive but illegal.

developer_response.body:
Thank you for taking the time to leave a review. We want to address your concerns thoughtfully and directly.

Here we are writing as the representative of the EvaAI app regarding this user review posted on the Meta platform, which makes serious allegations about our product's compliance with U.S. law and its licensure status. 

I believe this review violates both U.S. legal norms relating to defamation and unfair competition, as well as the Meta platform’s own Community Standards and content guidelines.

1. Defamation and Dissemination of False or Misleading Legal Claims

The review accuses EvaAI of engaging in illegal activity and practicing psychotherapy without a license, citing specific statutes and claiming our app operates unlawfully in the United States. These are serious legal allegations made in a public forum by a user who, to our knowledge, is not a regulatory authority and does not present proof or official regulatory action. Under U.S. law, specifically:

- Defamation Law: Making public, unsubstantiated claims that a business is breaking the law constitutes defamation, provided such statements are false and damage the business’s reputation, as outlined in common law and codified in multiple states (e.g., Restatement (Second) of Torts §§558, 559).
- Tortious Interference: False statements made to drive customers away from a business and harm its economic standing may constitute tortious interference with business relationships.

2. Unfair Competition and Potential Conflict of Interest

If the reviewer has any affiliation with a competing product or business, this review may also constitute unfair competition under the Lanham Act, 15 U.S.C. §1125(a), which prohibits false or misleading representation or descriptions that disparage another business.

3. Violation of Meta Platform Policies

According to Meta’s Community Standards and content policies, content that contains:

- Unsubstantiated accusations and legal threats,
- Attempts to harass, intimidate, or harm a business’s reputation for competitive gain,
- Misinformation or deceptive statements about the nature and legal status of services,

is not permitted and is subject to removal.

4. Lack of Regulatory Complaint or Legal Action

To our knowledge, there has been no regulatory investigation, official warning, or legal proceeding initiated against EvaAI for the issues raised in the review. The app does not represent itself as a replacement for licensed therapy; it provides general wellness and self-improvement tools, with appropriate disclaimers about their scope and limitations. The review, therefore, contains misleading and unsupported legal accusations.

If you or others have specific concerns, we welcome them through proper channels. We are always open to improvement and dialogue—but we also take our responsibility to uphold our team’s integrity seriously. Thank you again for your time and for exploring EvaAI.
